title: Unblocking a sink trap
subtitle: Plunge first, remove the trap if needed, clear and refit
excerpt: How to clear a blocked kitchen or bathroom sink: starting with a plunger, and if that fails, removing the U-bend trap under the sink, clearing the blockage, and refitting with a clean water seal.
sourceNotes: Standard UK domestic plumbing practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Blockage clearing guidance from CIPHE.

recipeTools.notes: For trap nuts that are too tight to undo by hand.
For blockages further down the waste pipe than the trap.

glossaryTerms.definition: A U-shaped or S-shaped section of waste pipe under a sink, basin, or bath, designed to hold a water seal that prevents drain odours rising. Unscrews for cleaning out blockages.

content.text: A slow or blocked kitchen sink is almost always caused by a build-up of grease and food residue in the U-bend
trap
under the sink. The trap is designed to be removed for clearing; the whole job is mechanical and needs no chemicals.
Step 1: try the plunger first

Block the overflow with a wet cloth (a blocked overflow means the plunger pressure bleeds away instead of driving down the pipe). Place the rubber cup of the plunger over the plughole, ensuring a complete seal. Push down firmly and pull up sharply, 10 to 15 times. The pressure wave loosens soft blockages near the plughole. Run the hot tap and check whether the water drains. If yes, done.
Removing the trap
Step 2, put a bowl under the trap.
The trap holds a water seal of about 300 to 500 ml; place a bucket or large bowl directly under it before loosening. Lay a towel to catch any spillage.
Step 3, unscrew the trap.

Modern plastic traps have large hand-turned cap nuts at each end of the U-bend. Unscrew both by hand anticlockwise. If stiff, use water-pump pliers but protect the plastic with a cloth. Lower the U-bend section and empty the water into the bucket.
Step 4, clear the trap.
Carry the trap to another sink or to the garden. Push a long handle or a bent wire through the trap to dislodge any solid blockage. Rinse under running water until the trap runs clear.
Step 5, check the waste pipe.

With the trap removed, look into the waste pipe stub that enters the wall. If the blockage is further down the pipe, push the drain snake into the pipe and rotate it while advancing, to cut through or pull out the obstruction.
Refitting
Step 6, reassemble the trap.

Check the rubber washer in each cap nut. If flat or perished, replace it. Refit the trap in the reverse order, hand-tightening the cap nuts. Do not overtighten plastic nuts; they crack. Run the hot tap for 30 seconds and check both cap nut joints for drips.
Common mistakes
Trap clears but sink blocks again quickly.

Grease build-up extends further into the waste pipe than the trap. Pour a kettle of boiling water followed by a washing-up liquid dose down the sink weekly for a few weeks to break down the grease accumulation.
Water dripping from the cap nut after refitting.
The washer is not seated flat in its groove. Undo the nut, re-seat the washer, and retighten.
